subject: Chemistry

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: What is the general molecular formula for a cycloalkane with a single ring and \(n\) carbon atoms, where \(n \ge 3\)?
questions.hint: Cycloalkanes are saturated but lose two hydrogens compared to acyclic alkanes.
questions.explanation: Cycloalkanes have the formula \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) because ring formation removes two hydrogens relative to acyclic alkanes (\(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n+2}}\)). Option B is for alkanes, option C is for alkynes.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: A cycloalkane is a saturated hydrocarbon with carbon atoms arranged in a ring, having the general formula \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Closing a chain into a ring uses two fewer hydrogen atoms than an open-chain alkane, resulting in the \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) formula.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a chain of carbons; connecting the ends removes two hydrogens, giving the ring formula.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(n\) carbons in a ring, each carbon contributes two hydrogens (except no terminal \(\mathrm{CH_3}\) groups), leading to \(2n\) hydrogens.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The degree of unsaturation (DoU) for a cycloalkane is 1, reflecting the ring. The formula \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) satisfies DoU = 1, while alkanes (\(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n+2}}\)) have DoU = 0.
reasoning_steps: Recall alkane formula: \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n+2}}\).
Recognize that forming a ring removes two H atoms.
Subtract 2: \(2n+2-2 = 2n\).
misconceptions: Confusing cycloalkanes with alkenes (both have \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) but different structures).

glossary_entry.definition: A saturated hydrocarbon containing a ring of carbon atoms.

flashcard.front: General formula for a single-ring cycloalkane?

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) is correct for cycloalkanes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.question: Which of the following is the simplest cycloalkane, containing only three carbon atoms in a ring?
questions.hint: The prefix indicates the number of carbons in the ring.
questions.explanation: Cyclopropane has three carbon atoms in a ring, satisfying the \(n \ge 3\) condition. Option B has four carbons, and C and D have five and six respectively.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES

explanations_tiered.definition: Cyclopropane is the smallest cycloalkane, with a three-carbon ring.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cycloalkanes require at least three carbons to form a ring. The naming reflects the ring size: cyclopropane (C3), cyclobutane (C4), etc.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cyclopropane is like a triangle of carbon atoms.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Minimum ring size for cycloalkanes is 3 (cyclopropane). Larger rings are named similarly (e.g., cyclopentane).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Cyclopropane has significant ring strain (Baeyer strain) due to 60° bond angles deviating from the ideal 109.5°, making it more reactive than other cycloalkanes.
reasoning_steps: Identify the prefix for 3 carbons: 'prop'.
Add the 'cyclo' prefix.
Select cyclopropane.
misconceptions: Thinking cycloalkanes can have 1 or 2 carbons (impossible for a stable ring).

glossary_entry.definition: The simplest cycloalkane, with three carbon atoms in a ring and the formula \(\mathrm{C_3H_6}\).

flashcard.front: Name the cycloalkane with three carbons.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Mention the stability difference between small and large rings.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Cyclopropane has three carbons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Which functional group characterizes an alkene, giving it the ending '-ene' in IUPAC names?
questions.hint: The suffix '-ene' indicates unsaturation with a double bond.
questions.explanation: Alkenes contain at least one carbon-carbon double bond (\(\mathrm{C=C}\)). Option A is for alkanes, and C is for alkynes.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: contain at least one carbon- carbon double bond

explanations_tiered.definition: An alkene is an unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The double bond (\(\mathrm{C=C}\)) is the functional group of alkenes, responsible for their reactivity and characteristic addition reactions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Alkenes have a 'double line' between two carbons.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The presence of a \(\pi\) bond in the \(\mathrm{C=C}\) unit makes alkenes more reactive than alkanes.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The double bond consists of a sigma (\(\sigma\)) and a pi (\(\pi\)) bond. The \(\pi\) bond is the reactive site for electrophilic addition.
reasoning_steps: Recall that '-ene' suffix indicates a double bond.
Match the description to the correct bond type.

glossary_entry.definition: An unsaturated hydrocarbon with at least one carbon-carbon double bond.

flashcard.front: What is the functional group of alkenes?

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The C=C bond is the alkene functional group.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the general formula for the alkene homologous series?
questions.hint: Alkenes have one double bond, reducing the hydrogen count by two compared to alkanes.
questions.explanation: Alkenes have the general formula \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) because the double bond reduces hydrogen count by 2 relative to alkanes. Option A is for alkanes, C for alkynes.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: The alkene general formula is \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) for molecules with one double bond and no rings.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The presence of one double bond reduces the hydrogen count by two compared to the saturated alkane formula.
explanations_tiered.beginner: For every \(n\) carbons, there are \(2n\) hydrogens.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since alkanes are \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n+2}}\), replacing a single bond with a double bond removes two hydrogens, giving \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The degree of unsaturation for a monounsaturated acyclic alkene is 1, corresponding to the formula \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the alkane formula.
Subtract 2 hydrogens for the double bond.
Identify the resulting formula.
misconceptions: Using the alkane formula for alkenes.

glossary_entry.definition: A formula representing the composition of a homologous series.

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\) is correct for alkenes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Why are alkenes classified as unsaturated hydrocarbons, while alkanes are classified as saturated?

options.B: Alkenes have the maximum number of hydrogen atoms possible
options.C: Alkenes contain a double bond and therefore have fewer hydrogen atoms than alkanes

questions.hint: Unsaturation refers to the presence of multiple bonds, which reduces hydrogen count.
questions.explanation: Unsaturated hydrocarbons contain double/triple bonds and fewer hydrogens than the maximum possible. Alkenes (C=C) have fewer H than alkanes (C-C). Option B describes saturated compounds.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Unsaturated hydrocarbons contain multiple bonds (double or triple) and thus fewer hydrogens than the maximum.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Saturation means maximum hydrogens (all single bonds). Alkenes have a double bond, so they lack the maximum possible hydrogens, making them unsaturated.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A saturated sponge is full of water; alkenes are 'unsaturated' because they are missing hydrogens and have a double bond.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The alkane formula is \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n+2}}\). An alkene with one double bond has formula \(\mathrm{C_nH_{2n}}\), missing 2 hydrogens.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Degree of unsaturation (DoU) = 0 for alkanes, = 1 for alkenes (or cycloalkanes). The \(\pi\) bond accounts for the unsaturation.
reasoning_steps: Recall that saturation = maximum H atoms.
Compare alkane (CnH2n+2) to alkene (CnH2n).
Conclude alkene has fewer H, hence unsaturated.
misconceptions: Thinking cyclic alkanes are unsaturated (they are saturated rings).

glossary_entry.definition: A hydrocarbon containing one or more carbon-carbon double or triple bonds.

flashcard.back: They contain a double bond, resulting in fewer hydrogens than the maximum possible.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Contains double/triple bonds, fewer H than saturated (e.g., alkenes/alkynes).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The state of having only single bonds.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Clarify that cycloalkanes are saturated despite formula CnH2n.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Double bond means fewer hydrogens.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the IUPAC name for the alkene \(\mathrm{CH_3CH=CHCH_2CH_3}\)?
questions.hint: Number the carbon chain to give the double bond the lowest possible number.
questions.explanation: The longest chain containing the double bond has 5 carbons (pentene). The double bond starts at carbon 2, so it is 2-pentene. Option A would require the double bond at C1.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: 2-Pentene is a five-carbon alkene with the double bond between carbons 2 and 3.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The parent chain is pentane changed to pentene. Numbering from the left gives the double bond at C2, right gives C3; thus C2 is correct.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find the longest carbon road that includes the double bridge, then count from the end closest to the bridge.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Chain: \(\mathrm{CH_3-CH=CH-CH_2-CH_3}\). The longest chain is 5 carbons. The double bond is between C2 and C3, yielding '2-pentene'.
explanations_tiered.advanced: IUPAC rules mandate the lowest locant for the double bond. The chain is numbered so that the first carbon of the double bond has the lower number, hence 2-pentene.
reasoning_steps: Identify the longest chain containing the C=C (5 C: pent).
Number from the end giving the double bond the lowest number (C2, not C3).
Name as 2-pentene.
misconceptions: Forgetting to include the double bond in the parent chain length.

glossary_entry.definition: Rules for naming alkenes: select longest chain with C=C, number to give C=C lowest number, suffix '-ene'.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Systematic naming rules for compounds with double bonds.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Number to give C=C lowest number.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The double bond is between C2 and C3 in a 5-carbon chain.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: What is the key difference between naming alkenes and naming alkanes in the IUPAC system?

options.A: Alkenes use the suffix '-ane' while alkanes use '-ene'
options.B: The longest chain must contain the double bond for alkenes
options.C: Alkenes do not use prefixes for substituents
options.D: Alkenes are always named as cycloalkanes

questions.hint: The functional group (double bond) must be in the parent chain.
questions.explanation: For alkenes, the parent chain must include the double bond, even if a longer chain without it exists. Option A is reversed; alkanes use '-ane', alkenes '-ene'.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: select the longest chain that contains the double bond

explanations_tiered.definition: IUPAC rules for alkenes prioritize the chain containing the double bond as the parent, even if a longer chain exists without it.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For alkanes, the longest chain overall is always the parent. For alkenes, the parent chain must contain the C=C functional group.
explanations_tiered.beginner: For alkenes, the double bond 'calls the shots'—the main chain must include it.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Example: \(\mathrm{CH_2=CH-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_3}\) is 1-hexene, not 1-pentene, even though a longer chain might exist? Actually, hexane chain includes double bond. Priority is inclusion of C=C.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Rule P-44.2 of IUPAC Blue Book: The principal chain of an alkene must include the double bond, taking precedence over chain length if necessary.
reasoning_steps: Recall alkane rule: longest chain wins.
Recall alkene rule: chain must include double bond.
Recognize this as the fundamental difference.
misconceptions: Assuming the alkane rule of 'longest chain overall' applies directly to alkenes.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Alkene parent chain = contains C=C (may not be longest if isolated).

glossary_entry.definition: The main chain used as the basis for naming an organic compound.

flashcard.front: How does alkene naming differ from alkane naming?
flashcard.back: The parent chain must contain the double bond, even if a longer chain without it exists.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Naming rule difference: alkane vs alkene?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Alkene parent chain must contain the double bond.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: In naming, the main chain must include the highest priority functional group.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The chain must include the double bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: How do the boiling points of alkenes compare to alkanes with the same number of carbon atoms?

options.A: Alkenes have much higher boiling points due to hydrogen bonding
options.B: Alkenes have slightly lower or similar boiling points due to similar London forces
options.C: Alkenes are always gases regardless of carbon count

questions.hint: Both alkanes and alkenes are nonpolar and experience London dispersion forces.
questions.explanation: Alkenes and alkanes are both nonpolar, so their boiling points are governed by London forces, which are similar for comparable molecular masses. Option A is false because alkenes cannot hydrogen bond.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Physical properties of alkenes are similar to alkanes because both are nonpolar hydrocarbons.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Alkenes and alkanes of similar molar mass have comparable London dispersion forces, hence similar boiling points.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like alkanes, alkenes are 'oily' and don't stick strongly to each other.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: C4 alkanes (butane, bp -0.5°C) vs C4 alkenes (1-butene, bp -6.3°C) are very close. Dispersion forces dominate.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The lack of polarity and inability to hydrogen bond means that the weak London forces are the primary intermolecular attraction, leading to low boiling points.
reasoning_steps: Identify the intermolecular force present (London).
Recognize that mass/surface area determines strength.
Compare alkanes and alkenes of similar mass (similar forces).
misconceptions: Assuming double bonds lead to stronger attractions (they don't significantly increase polarity).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Alkene BP ≈ Alkane BP (same C count).

glossary_entry.definition: Similar to alkanes: insoluble in water, low density, boiling points increase with chain length.

flashcard.front: Alkene vs Alkane boiling points (same C count)?
flashcard.back: Similar; both nonpolar with London forces.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. London forces are comparable.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Boiling point trend: alkenes vs alkanes?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Weak intermolecular forces in nonpolar molecules.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Similar mass = similar London forces.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: Which type of isomerism is unique to alkenes due to the restricted rotation around the carbon-carbon double bond?
questions.hint: Restricted rotation leads to different spatial arrangements.
questions.explanation: Cis-trans isomerism arises because the double bond prevents free rotation, allowing different geometric orientations. Options A and B occur in alkanes as well, but C is specific to alkenes (and cycloalkanes).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons

explanations_tiered.definition: Geometrical (cis-trans) isomerism is a form of stereoisomerism resulting from restricted rotation in alkenes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The \(\pi\) bond in alkenes locks the two carbon atoms in place, allowing different arrangements of substituents (cis = same side, trans = opposite).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The double bond acts like a stiff rod; you can't spin the two halves, so groups can be 'up' or 'down'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In 2-butene, the two methyl groups can be on the same side (cis) or opposite sides (trans), leading to distinct physical properties.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The energy barrier for rotation around a double bond is approximately 250 kJ/mol, effectively locking the geometry at room temperature and producing stable isomers.
reasoning_steps: Recall that single bonds allow rotation, double bonds do not.
Recognize that 'same side' vs 'opposite' defines cis/trans.
Identify this as a unique geometric isomerism.
misconceptions: Thinking all alkenes show cis-trans isomerism (requires two different groups on each C of the double bond).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cis-trans isomerism = due to restricted rotation around C=C.

glossary_entry.definition: Isomerism due to the different spatial arrangement of atoms around a rigid bond, such as a double bond.

flashcard.front: What type of isomerism is specific to alkenes (due to C=C)?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Cis-trans isomerism is unique to systems with restricted rotation.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What isomerism is caused by restricted rotation around a double bond?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Stereoisomerism in alkenes where substituents differ in spatial arrangement due to the rigid double bond.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Geometric isomerism is unique to alkenes (due to C=C).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
questions.section: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
questions.question: For which alkene does cis-trans isomerism NOT occur, despite the presence of a double bond?
questions.hint: Cis-trans isomerism requires two different groups on each carbon of the double bond.
questions.explanation: 1-Butene has two hydrogens on carbon 1 of the double bond, making geometric isomerism impossible. Option A, 2-butene, has CH3 and H on each carbon, so cis/trans is possible.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 6: HYDROCARBONS AND THEIR NATURAL SOURCES
source_trace.section_anchor: 6.3 Unsaturated Hydrocarbons: Alkenes, Alkynes, and Aromatic Hydrocarbons
source_trace.content_anchor: alkenes cannot have geometrical isomers if two identical groups are attached to any one of the carbon atoms

explanations_tiered.definition: Cis-trans isomerism requires each carbon of the double bond to have two different substituents.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In 1-butene, the first carbon has two identical H atoms, so swapping them does not change the geometry; thus no cis/trans.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If one side of the double bond has two of the same thing (like two H's), there is no 'same side' vs 'opposite side'.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Condition for cis-trans: \(\mathrm{C_a} \) must have A ≠ B, and \(\mathrm{C_b}\) must have C ≠ D.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For 1-butene (\(\mathrm{H_2C=CHCH_2CH_3}\)), one alkene carbon has \(\mathrm{H}\) and \(\mathrm{H}\) (identical), so no stereoisomers exist.
reasoning_steps: Check each carbon of the double bond.
Determine if both carbons have two different substituents.
If any carbon has two identical groups, cis-trans is impossible.
misconceptions: Assuming all alkenes exhibit cis-trans isomerism.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cis-trans requires: C=C with 4 different groups total across the two carbons? Actually: each C must have 2 different groups.

glossary_entry.definition: Both carbons of the double bond must have two different groups attached for geometric isomers to exist.

flashcard.back: One carbon of the double bond has two identical hydrogens.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. 1-Butene has \(=CH_2\), so no cis/trans.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An alkene with the double bond at the end of the chain (\(=CH_2\)).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Terminal alkene (C1 has two H's).
